turns.text: Hello Katelin, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. Honestly, it’s a bit surreal to be on this side of the microphone. I spent so much of my life reading journals and analyzing outcome data, thinking I was building a shield of knowledge. I thought if I understood the mechanics of pre-eclampsia—if I could recite the systolic and diastolic thresholds—I’d be in control. 

But when I was sitting in that exam room in Bridgewater, watching the specialist’s eyes flicker over my chart instead of meeting my gaze, the research didn't protect me. It just made the terror precise. I wasn't just a scared mom; I was a scared mom who knew exactly how quickly things could go sideways. 

The hardest part wasn't even the diagnosis itself; it was the way the providers talked to me. They’d use these simplified, patronizing tones, like I was some naive kid who didn’t understand what a NICU stay would mean. I wanted to scream, "I know the percentages! I’ve seen the mortality curves!" I didn't need them to water it down. I needed them to see me—not just as a "case" of high-risk hypertension, but as a person who was terrified for her child. It’s been weeks, and I’m still trying to untangle the exhaustion from the trauma of it all. Where would you like to start?
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

To be completely honest, I think I deluded myself. In my research, I focused so much on fetal outcomes and the pathology of the complications that I treated my own body as an afterthought—like it was just the vessel for the pregnancy, rather than a system that was actually going through a major medical event. 

I expected to feel "normal" quickly. I thought if I just followed the post-op instructions to the letter, my blood pressure would stabilize and I could go back to being the person I was before. I anticipated the standard physical healing—the exhaustion, the soreness—but I completely underestimated the physiological "hangover" of pre-eclampsia. My body felt like a total stranger. I’d look at my legs, still swollen, and feel this intense, irrational anger. It was a physical reminder of the hospital, of the monitors, of the fear. 

The reality was that the physical recovery wasn't just about my incision or my blood pressure. It was the crushing weight of the anxiety that didn't leave when I left the ward. My body was still "on" for a threat that wasn't there anymore. It’s hard to heal when your nervous system is still expecting a crash-cart to roll into your living room. I expected to be back on my feet; I didn't realize I’d be crawling out of a trauma response.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

It’s the silence. Nobody tells you about the sound of your own heartbeat becoming an obsession. During my recovery, I became hyper-aware of my blood pressure in a way that’s almost pathological. Because I knew the data, I’d sit here in my apartment—my baby sleeping right there—and instead of feeling peaceful, I’d be mentally calculating my Mean Arterial Pressure. It’s like my body developed this phantom limb, only instead of a limb, it’s a cuff squeezing my arm.

The other thing is the constant, nagging feeling of "fragility." People talk about postpartum physical recovery in terms of healing wounds or losing weight, but they don’t talk about the cardiovascular fallout. My providers act like everything is "resolved" because I’m technically discharged, but I can feel that my system is still delicate. Every time I get a headache, even a minor one from lack of sleep, my heart starts racing because I can’t help but think about the stroke risk or the long-term hypertension markers I spent years reading about. It’s not just a physical sensation; it’s an intellectual one, too. The medical community seems to view the "patient" as someone who doesn't know their own biology. I know mine too well, and I think that’s why I can’t just let the recovery happen. I’m constantly analyzing it.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I thought feeding would be the one thing I could truly own. In my mind, it was purely biological, a way to reclaim some of the agency I felt I’d lost to the medical machinery of the hospital. I told myself it would be this quiet, bonding anchor that would help me reset. I didn't worry about the "difficulty"—I’m a researcher, I’m good at following protocols and learning new systems. I figured if there was a problem, I’d just troubleshoot it like a clinical challenge.

But the reality was completely dismantled by my own trauma. Because of the pre-eclampsia and the constant monitoring, I felt like my body had "failed" to keep my baby inside long enough to be perfectly safe. Every time I went to feed, I wasn't just feeding a baby; I was looking at this tiny, fragile human and feeling an overwhelming sense of guilt. Was my milk "good enough" to make up for the early birth? Was I stressed enough to impact the quality? 

The physical exhaustion was so deep it felt like my bones were heavy. I expected support, but it felt like the lactation consultants were just another set of experts telling me what to do, which triggered that same defensive feeling I had with my OB. I didn't need another lecture on latch techniques; I needed someone to acknowledge that I was terrified I might fail at this, too. It wasn't the peaceful, bonding experience the brochures promised; it was just one more data point in a life that felt like it was slipping out of my control.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I fell for the romanticized version of "the glow" that everyone talks about, or at the very least, I expected that once the baby was safely in my arms, the anxiety would just... dissipate. I’d spent months bracing for a disaster, so I assumed that once the danger was theoretically behind us, my brain would just flip a switch to "joy." I expected to be overwhelmed, sure, but in that "new mom" sort of way—the kind you see in social media posts, messy but happy.

The reality hit me like a physical blow. The anxiety didn't vanish; it just changed shape. Before birth, it was directed at the clinical outcomes—the numbers, the charts, the fetal heart rate. After, it became this pervasive, suffocating sense of hyper-vigilance. I don't "rest" when the baby sleeps; I listen. I listen for breathing, for movement, for anything that suggests the fragility I documented in my research is playing out right in front of me.

It’s an incredibly lonely kind of PTSD. I feel like I’m constantly mourning the pregnancy I was "supposed" to have—a healthy, boring one where I wasn't just a clinical variable. I find myself getting angry at the simplest things, like when a friend tells me to "just relax." They don't understand that I’m not just a mom; I’m a researcher who knows the worst-case scenarios, and I can't just un-know them. I feel like I'm grieving a version of myself that existed before I learned how much can go wrong.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That is the exact tension I live with every single day. There is this societal script that says if you have a healthy baby, you should be nothing but grateful—that anything less is a failure of character. Professionals, my family, even the people at the grocery store... they all look at the baby, then at me, and their faces are just beaming. They expect me to mirror that joy. If I mention that I’m struggling or that I’m still terrified, I see that uncomfortable flicker in their eyes. They want to rush to "it could have been worse," as if that’s a comfort instead of a confirmation of my trauma.

And the professionals? They’re the worst because they view the "successful" delivery as the finish line. I’m a "success story." My baby is here. They’ve closed my file. But I am still very much in the middle of the narrative. When I try to explain my lingering fear or the way I’m dissecting my health, they treat it like a minor postpartum adjustment rather than the result of a medical system that stripped away my personhood. 

I feel like I’m not allowed to be angry or haunted because "the baby survived." I’m holding this enormous, complex grief that I’m supposed to keep quiet because it ruins the happy ending everyone else is celebrating. It feels like I’m performing a role that doesn't actually fit the reality of what happened to me.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I always saw myself as the one who *analyzed* the world—the objective observer, the academic, the woman with a clear career trajectory in perinatal research. I expected motherhood to be an addition to that identity, a new chapter in a book I was already writing. I thought I’d be the "informed mom," using my data literacy to navigate the pediatric world with precision and confidence.

But the reality is that the identity I built so carefully—my intellect, my career focus, my sense of competence—was essentially scorched earth by the experience of my pregnancy. When you spend months being treated as a collection of symptoms and elevated blood pressure readings, you lose the sense of being a person with a history and a future. 

Now, I feel like I’m in this strange, liminal space. I’m a researcher who is terrified of the very data she used to respect. I’m a single mother in a small apartment who feels like she’s living in a medical textbook. I don't feel like the ambitious young professional I was; I feel like a survivor of a system that didn't know how to see me. I’m constantly struggling to reconcile the "clinical" version of me with the "mother" version of me. They don't quite fit together yet, and I’m not sure they ever will.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

My professional identity is the hardest one to keep a grip on, and that’s the irony of it all. I spent years in those clinics and labs looking at the "big picture" of maternal outcomes, trying to find those statistical gains that make a difference for thousands of families. Now, when I look at a research paper or even think about my old desk, it feels like I’m looking at an artifact from a different civilization. 

It’s hard to hold onto the "researcher" part of me because that version of Katelin believed in the power of medicine to fix things. She believed that if you worked hard enough and understood the mechanisms, you could achieve a positive outcome. I’ve seen the "positive outcome"—my beautiful baby—but I’ve also seen the hidden cost of the process. How do I go back to analyzing data sets when I know the visceral, trembling fear of the person behind those numbers? Every time I try to engage with that world, I just feel this deep, hollow anger. It’s like the academic rigor I valued is now a constant reminder of how dehumanizing the clinical process actually is.

I feel like I’ve lost the luxury of being an observer. I’m not just studying the data anymore; I’m the data. And that’s a heavy, lonely place to be.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a space for decompression. I thought, *Finally, someone is going to sit down and walk me through what actually happened.* I wanted them to acknowledge the trauma, to explain the "why" behind the emergency decisions, and to check in on how I was coping—not just my blood pressure, but my brain. I expected a partnership, a transition from the crisis-management of the hospital back into a human-centered relationship with my providers.

In reality? It was entirely mechanical. My appointments felt like a quick assembly line. I’d walk in, they’d strap that cuff on my arm, check the incision, ask if I was breastfeeding, and move on. It was all about the metrics: Is the wound closed? Is the BP under control? It was like they were checking to see if the "case" was successfully closed. When I tried to bring up the anxiety or the way I was feeling, they’d offer a quick, practiced, "It’s very common, you're doing great," before moving on to the next patient.

They completely bypassed the emotional wreckage. It felt like they were managing the symptoms of a machine, not the recovery of a mother. It was just more of the same—being treated like a data point that had finally returned to the "normal" range. Every time I left those appointments, I felt more invisible than when I walked in.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

It’s the recurring theme of my life: the difference between "medically stable" and "mentally functional." I tried to bring up the intrusive, racing thoughts I have at night—the way I compulsively check the baby’s vitals and track my own health markers. I tried to tell them that my anxiety wasn't just "baby blues," that it felt like my brain was stuck in a feedback loop from the hospital. 

Their response was dismissive, almost clinical. They’d look at their screens, nod, and say, "It’s a big adjustment, Katelin. You're a new mom, you're tired, it’s normal." They’d check off a box on a depression screening form and think they’d done their due diligence. But that form doesn't capture the nuance of someone who knows *too much* about the risks. 

When I tried to push, to explain that my background in research was actually making this harder because I couldn't "un-see" the complications, they’d smile that condescending smile and say I should try to "stay off the internet." It made me feel so isolated. They weren't listening to the content of what I was saying; they were just listening to the frequency of a "stressed young woman." I needed a partner who would help me process the medical trauma, but instead, I just got treated like I was overreacting. It made me feel like I had to stop talking to them entirely, which just left me even more alone in this apartment.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

Returning to work was something I approached with a strange mix of desperate ambition and complete dread. I thought it would be my lifeline—a way to reclaim the "Katelin" who existed before the monitors and the blood pressure spikes. I assumed that if I could just get back into a workspace, surrounded by intellectual challenges and professional peers, I would finally feel like a functioning, competent adult again instead of just a scared patient. I expected that the structure of a workday would force my brain to stop cycling through those traumatic, clinical "what-ifs."

The reality was a total shock to my system. Walking back into that environment felt like stepping onto a different planet. My colleagues would ask, "How are you?" with that polite, surface-level expectation that I’d say, "Great, the baby is doing well." But I wasn't doing well. I was constantly battling a fog of exhaustion so deep it felt like I was wading through water, all while dealing with that lingering, low-grade physical instability. 

And then there was the anger. Every time a meeting topic touched on prenatal care or NICU research, I had to physically restrain myself from shaking. My peers were talking about "optimizing patient outcomes" while I was sitting there, still feeling the phantom pressure of a cuff on my arm. It wasn't the distraction I needed; it was a constant, painful reminder of everything that had been taken from me. I realized that the "professional" version of me had been changed, and trying to force myself back into that old mold felt like trying to put on clothes that didn't fit anymore. I didn't feel empowered; I felt exposed.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

Honestly, the sheer friction of it is what drains me. You have this image of yourself being the high-functioning professional, but in reality, I’m spending half my workday in a frantic calculation of logistical trade-offs. I’m a single mom, so there is no "off-loading" the mental load here; it’s all on me. 

The biggest hurdle is the complete incompatibility between the pace of my work and the pace of recovery. I’m trying to focus on data, but my body is constantly reminding me of its limits—a dull throb in my head, the lingering fatigue that doesn't just go away because I’m sitting at a desk. And then there’s the feeding schedule. Trying to manage pumping in a professional setting, especially when you’re already feeling scrutinized as a "young, unmarried mom," is its own layer of exhausting performance. I feel like I’m constantly hiding the fact that I’m struggling to keep my head above water.

Childcare is the heavy, unspoken anchor. Every time I leave the baby, I’m fighting the urge to run back because my brain is still stuck in that "high-risk" loop, imagining every possible catastrophic outcome. It’s like I have to compartmentalize my terror just to finish a spreadsheet. It’s not just about managing time; it’s about managing a nervous system that is fundamentally wired to believe that being away from the baby—or being back at work—is a massive safety risk. It’s physically and mentally grueling.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I’m a single mother, so there is no partner to share the burden of the night feeds, the anxiety, or the logistical nightmare of a medical crisis. When I look at other couples—at the way they trade off holding the baby or divide the mental load of scheduling appointments—I’m hit with this sharp, hollow ache. I don't just feel the exhaustion of being a new mom; I feel the crushing weight of being the sole witness to my own trauma.

Before this, I think I romanticized the idea of "doing it alone." I thought my resilience, my research background, and my drive would be enough to compensate for not having someone else in the trenches with me. I figured I’d be this self-contained unit, perfectly balancing the baby and my life. But the reality is that I’m drowning in the silence of this apartment. There is no one to look at and say, "Did you see that?" or "I’m terrified right now." 

There’s no "we" to lean on. When the pre-eclampsia took hold and the doctors were talking down to me, I was the only one in the room to process that dehumanization. Being a single mom after a high-risk birth means carrying the memory of the trauma entirely on my own shoulders. It makes the world feel smaller, colder, and significantly more dangerous. I didn't realize that the hardest part of "doing it alone" wouldn't be the tasks—it would be the absolute, suffocating loneliness of having no one to share the burden of the fear.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

Since I’m a single mother, "partnership" isn't a dynamic I have with a romantic partner, but I’ve been surprised by how much I’ve had to invent one—or, more accurately, how much I’ve had to fill that void with an imagined, often critical, internal narrator. 

The biggest surprise is that I’ve become my own harshest "partner." In the absence of someone else to help share the load or validate the fear, I’ve started treating myself like a research subject that isn't meeting the benchmarks. I find myself constantly evaluating my performance as a mother against the hypothetical "ideal" partner I imagined I’d be. If I’m exhausted, I’m not just tired; I’m "failing to optimize my recovery." If I’m anxious, I’m "over-analyzing." 

I expected that I’d be self-sufficient, but I didn't realize how much the lack of a partner would force me into this hyper-critical, almost clinical relationship with myself. There’s no one to hold the space for my vulnerability, so I’ve locked it away behind a wall of "I can do this." It’s lonely in a way that feels like a physical ache. I’m the only person in this apartment who knows the full history of my birth, and that keeps me trapped in a loop of silent, self-imposed judgment. I’m not just the parent; I’m the witness, the advocate, and the judge, all at once.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected a partnership—a collaborative, clinical alliance where my intellect was a tool, not a burden. I thought that by being "informed," I would gain some semblance of agency. I expected that even if things went wrong, I would be guided through the trauma by someone who could see the person behind the blood pressure charts. I thought the medical system would be a safety net that caught me when the biology faltered.

The gap—the canyon, really—between that expectation and reality is the complete stripping away of my personhood. I received efficient, cold, and transactional care that prioritized the "case" over the human. They managed the pre-eclampsia, yes, and I am grateful for that, but they utterly failed to manage me. They didn't see a researcher with agency; they saw a young, single woman to be managed, spoken down to, and ultimately discharged.

The most profound gap is that I expected to be treated as the protagonist of my own medical journey, but instead, I was a background variable. I left the hospital with a baby, but I also left with a deep, silent trauma that no one cared to address because the "data" looked good. They fixed the body, but they shattered the person, and that is a gap that no amount of standard post-birth care has ever tried to bridge.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would fundamentally overhaul the "handoff" between clinical, acute care and the postpartum period. Right now, the system is designed to treat pregnancy and birth as these discrete, time-bound events that "end" the moment you walk out of the hospital doors. It treats the postpartum months as a period where the body just naturally resets itself, provided the physical indicators are in the "normal" range. 

If I could change the design, it would be to implement a "continuity of personhood" model. I would move away from this assembly-line check-up structure and replace it with a model that assigns a dedicated, trauma-informed care coordinator to patients—especially those with high-risk diagnoses—who stays with them for at least the first six months. This person wouldn't just be checking my blood pressure; they would be checking the *person*. 

The system treats maternity care like a manufacturing process—get the baby out safely, ensure the mother isn't actively hemorrhaging, and move on. It ignores the neurological and psychological imprint of medical trauma. I would build a system that acknowledges that a high-risk birth is a major life-altering event, not just a technicality. It would involve real, meaningful psychological support that is integrated into the routine, not treated as an "extra" or a "referral" that I have to fight to get. They need to stop looking at the monitors and start looking at the mother.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that still sits with me—the thing that keeps me awake when everything else is finally quiet—is the way my intelligence was treated as an inconvenience. Throughout the entire ordeal, from the first elevated reading to my postpartum check-ups, there was this subtle, persistent messaging that if I knew too much, I was just "making myself anxious." They acted like my background in research was a character flaw, something that needed to be managed, rather than a resource that could have been used to build a real partnership.

If someone had just stopped, looked at my chart, and then actually looked at me—*really* looked at me—and said, "I know you understand the data, Katelin. Let’s talk about it," it would have changed everything. It wouldn't have made the pre-eclampsia go away, but it would have kept me from feeling so fundamentally alone in the middle of a medical crisis. 

Being a mother isn’t just a biological output; it’s a life-shattering transformation. I think the system needs to understand that we aren't just "cases" to be resolved. We are human beings carrying the weight of what happened to us, and we deserve to be treated with the dignity of someone who is an expert in her own life, even when that life feels like it’s falling apart. Thank you for listening. It feels... lighter, just to say it out loud.
